original_abstract:
While artificial intelligence (AI) has the potential to drive significant socio-economic transformation across Africa, its development and deployment remain largely dominated by the Global North, often mirroring historical colonial patterns of resource extraction. This paper examines the critical landscape of Responsible AI within the African context, identifying the systemic challenges that hinder equitable technological integration. Key barriers discussed include significant gaps in digital literacy, inadequate physical and internet infrastructure, price barriers to mobile connectivity, and a scarcity of localized datasets and specialized AI talent. 

The study provides a comprehensive survey of over 100 AI startups and research organizations across the continent, revealing a nascent but rapidly growing ecosystem concentrated in regions like Nigeria and South Africa. However, this growth is accompanied by emerging concerns, particularly regarding the export of surveillance technologies and "authoritarian tech" by foreign entities, which risk exacerbating political repression and racial bias. By defining the core tenets of Responsible AI—accountability, transparency, explicability, and the mitigation of bias—through an African lens, the authors offer actionable recommendations for policymakers, researchers, and global tech actors. The paper concludes that for AI to serve as a viable pathway toward economic freedom, its evolution must be "for Africans by Africans," supported by robust regulatory frameworks that prioritize local values and protect citizen autonomy.
humanized_abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI) holds a lot of promise for transformative socio-economic development in Africa. However, the AI value chain is skewed in favour of the Global North, perpetuating a resource extraction model reminiscent of colonialism. This paper interrogates the Responsible AI landscape in Africa, and diagnoses the challenges that are presently thwarting AI-based innovation and equitable development on the continent. These include inadequate digital skills, infrastructure, both physical and digital, expensive mobile services, and a paucity of local AI-related training data and skilled workforce.

This report provides a first-ever survey of more than 100 African companies and organisations engaged in AI innovation and research. While the industry is still in its infancy in Africa, it is expanding fast, particularly in Nigeria and South Africa, and presents the prospect of spreading the benefits and ownership of new technologies on the continent. However, global developments in this respect raise several warning flags – notably the transfer of surveillance technology and so-called “authoritarian tech” from other regions to Africa. To ensure that the development of AI on the continent follows a responsible and humane path the authors use an African perspective to define the basic principles of Responsible AI—accountability, transparency, explainability and reduction of bias—while providing practical recommendations for governments, academics and companies that work on and with AI technologies on the continent. The aim is to ensure that innovation in the AI sector also fosters the prosperity and economic empowerment of the peoples of Africa, and is therefore developed in African countries for the benefit of all, within the framework of appropriate governance and legislation reflecting the cultures of these countries, while at the same time safeguarding the individual rights of its citizens.
